segments.s378-g81: خردمند شد is composed of the adjective/noun خردمند ('wise') plus شد, the past of شدن ('to become'), so it means 'he became wise.' The clause نامه شاه برد follows without an explicit subject or conjunction — نامه شاه is a possessive noun phrase ('the king's letter', with the possessor after the possessed) and برد is the past tense of بردن ('took'); the omitted subject is the same as for شد (pro-drop). The two short clauses are coordinated by sequence (common in poetry) to mean successive actions.
segments.s378-g82: به marks the indirect object ('to/for'), so به تازنده کوه و بیابان introduces the recipient: تازنده is an active participle used as a noun ('the one who hastens/rapid rider'), and کوه و بیابان ('mountain and desert') follows as a coordinated modifier describing where he rides; note the poetic omission of an ezāfe. سپرد is the simple past of سپردن ('entrusted/handed over') and takes the previously mentioned نامه as its direct object, so the whole phrase means 'he entrusted (it) to the swift rider of mountain and plain.'
segments.s378-g65: بدو is a prepositional/dative form meaning 'to him' (marks the indirect object). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech). شو is an imperative 'become/go' directing the addressee. پیش + name is a prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before Esfandiyar.'
segments.s378-g66: بخوان is an imperative 'summon' or 'call' (read/summon). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. مر + او را functions datively/benefactively 'bring him to me' (مر = 'to me / for me', او را marks the person). به ره باش یار is an instruction: به ره 'on the road' (prepositional phrase), باش is an imperative 'be', and یار here means 'companion/ally' — 'be his companion on the way.'
segments.s378-g67: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic ش), and که introduces the content clause. برخیز is an imperative 'stand up / arise'. و links the next command. نزد من آی is 'come near me' (نزد = 'in the presence of/near', من = 'me', آی = imperative 'come').
segments.s378-g68: چو is a temporal subordinate particle 'when'. نامه is the object 'the letter'. بخوانی is 2nd-person singular (subjunctive/temporal) 'you read' (used for 'when you read'). به ره is a locative 'on/along the road'. بر is the preposition/particle 'on/upon/toward' linking to the following verb. مپای is an older negative/imperative form meaning 'do not set foot / do not advance' (poetic/archaic); here it forbids moving on the road at that time.
segments.s378-g69: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. کاری here is a noun 'a deed / an undertaking' (the provided senses do not match this usage). بزرگست is the copular+adjective 'is great' (archaic fused form). پیش اندرا = 'in the presence of / before Indra' (پیش as preposition + proper name).
segments.s378-g70: تو is the 2nd-person singular subject 'you'. پایی ('by/with the foot' or 'step') here expresses instrumentality — 'by your step/foot'. همی is an older particle that adds emphasis or marks a modal/ongoing sense ('will / indeed'). این همه کشورا is the object phrase 'all these countries/lands' (این همه = 'all these', کشورا = plural 'lands'). The clause means roughly 'you will, by your step, cover all these lands.'
segments.s378-g71: یکی کار is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain task / one affair'. اکنون is the adverb 'now'. همی is an emphatic/auxiliary particle (classical usage). بایدا is an archaic modal verb 'must / ought to' (here stating necessity).
segments.s378-g72: که introduces the content clause 'that'. بی‌تو is a prepositional phrase 'without you'. چنین کار is 'such a deed / work'. برنایدا is an older negative/potential form meaning 'cannot be done / will not happen' — here it asserts that the deed cannot be accomplished without the addressed person.
segments.s378-g73: نوشته is a noun 'writing/inscription' serving as the object; نوشتش is a past verbal form with enclitic -ش (‚he wrote it/for him') and here literally ties the action to that writing — together they mean 'he wrote the writing' (a common poetic doubling). یکی استوار is a noun phrase modifying that writing: یکی = 'one/a single' and استوار = 'firm/steady', so the whole segment says 'he wrote one firm inscription.'
segments.s378-g74: که here introduces the content of the inscription ('that / which') and the following ای (contained in the orthography as که‌ای) is the vocative 'O' addressing the addressee. نامور فرخ اسفندیار is the direct vocative name-cluster: نامور 'renowned', فرخ 'fortunate/happy', اسفندیار the proper name.
segments.s378-g75: فرستادم is first-person past 'I sent.' این پیر جاماسپ را is the direct object with demonstrative + epithet + name, finalized by the object marker را; literally 'I sent this old Jamasp.' The object marker را marks the noun as definite/accusative.
segments.s378-g76: که introduces the subordinate clause of purpose/content ('to/that'); دستور is 'order/command'; بد qualifies it as 'bad/harsh' (here: stern or adverse); شاه لهراسپ را is 'King Lohrasp' with را again marking the definite object of دستور (the order belonging to Lohrasp). The clause literally reads 'that (he should) [deliver/execute] King Lohrasp's harsh order.'
segments.s378-g77: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; او را 'him' with object marker را; ببینی is the present/subjunctive form 'you see/meet' used here as 'when you see him'; میان را 'the middle/central way' with را marks it as definite object; ببند is an imperative 'close/shut.' The sequence gives the instruction: 'When you see him, shut the middle (passage).'
segments.s378-g78: ابا here carries the meaning 'do not be reluctant / do not hesitate' (classical usage allowing an exhortation); او 'him'; بیا is an imperative 'bring/come' (here 'bring'); بر 'onto/on' and ستور 'steed/horse' specify how to bring him; نوند is a proper name (the horse's name). Together: 'do not hesitate — bring him onto the steed Navand.'
segments.s378-g79: اگر 'if'; خفته‌ای is a stative/past-participial form with 2nd person ending 'you are/have slept' — here 'if you are asleep'; زود 'quickly' (adverb); برجه 'turret/tower' (destination); به پای 'on foot' or 'to the foot' (manner/place). The clause issues the quick-action instruction: 'If you are asleep, quickly to the turret on foot.'
segments.s378-g80: وگر 'but if / otherwise'; خود 'yourself' (emphatic); بپایی is a present/subjunctive/imperative sense 'you rise/stand up' (from 'بپا'); زمانی 'for a time / for a moment' (here 'even a moment'); مپای is an archaic/poetic negative imperative meaning 'do not wait/linger.' Thus: 'but if you can rise yourself, do not wait even a moment.'
segments.s378-g49: چنو is a subordinating adverb meaning 'when/as' (here 'when'). حلقه کرد is a light verb construction: حلقه (a circle/loop) + کرد (past 'made'), so 'made a ring'. آن کمند is the topical noun phrase 'that lasso/snare' (آن = 'that' before the noun). بتاب is an archaic/poetic verb form meaning 'to be agitated/whirl' (imperf./present sense) describing the motion of the snare; word order places the verb after the topical object, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s378-g50: This short clause gives the effect of the action: پذیره here means 'shelter/refuge' or 'admission' (i.e. allowance of entrance). شدن is the verbal infinitive/auxiliary 'to become / to be(come)'; آفتاب is 'the sun'. نیارد in the manuscript is an archaic/irregular form here conveying 'does not allow / does not admit' (negative sense), so the whole phrase means roughly 'it did not admit the sun' or 'it would not let the sun enter.' Word order is noun + (archaic) negative-verb + شدن + subject noun.
segments.s378-g51: کنون is a fronted temporal adverb 'now'. از شنیده is an ablative/agentive phrase 'from what (one) has heard' or 'based on what I heard'. بگفتمت is a past perfective verb with enclitic object 'ت' — 'I told you' (بـ- prefix + گفت + مت). راست is a postposed adverb/adjective meaning 'true' or 'truly' and comments on the preceding clause: 'I truly told you (this).' The clause is compacted in poetic word order but is a straightforward past assertion.
segments.s378-g52: This is an imperative/assertive exhortation. تو is the 2nd singular pronoun 'you'. به دان is an exhortative phrase 'know / be aware of' where دان is an imperative/infinitive of 'to know' and به introduces the verb in an instructive phrase. کنون 'now' again temporal. رای و فرمان is a compound noun phrase 'counsel and command' (رای = plan/opinion/counsel; فرمان = command). تراست is an archaic fused form (= ترا + است) meaning 'is yours' or 'belongs to you'. Overall: 'Know now that counsel and command belong to you.'
segments.s378-g53: چو is a conditional conjunction 'if'. با شاه ایران is an instrument/associate phrase 'with the Shah of Iran' (با + noun). گرزم is a 1st-person-present/intentional verb form from گرز زدن 'to strike/beat with a mace' (here 'I strike / I wield my mace'). این براند is the result clause: این = 'this' (the action or consequence just mentioned) and براند is subjunctive/future-like 'will drive away' (راندن → براند). The clause is a protasis/apodosis pair typical of Persian conditional poetry.
segments.s378-g54: گو is an imperative or quotative 'say' (used to introduce reported or hortatory speech). نامبردار is an epithet 'the named/aforementioned or famed one' used here vocatively or as subject. خیره بماند is a stative optical construction: خیره 'staring, astonished' + بماند (subjunctive/optative of ماندن) 'may remain' or simply 'remains/stays', so together 'remains staring/astonished.' The order (verb-final) is typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s378-g55: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said' — here a narrative perfect). هرگز introduces negation 'never' and is followed by که + verb clause which here functions as a free relative/complement meaning 'that he had seen' or elliptically 'one had seen.' دید این شگفت = 'seen this marvel/wonder.' The sequence marks reported speech followed by the speaker's reactionive comment.
segments.s378-g56: This is two short coordinated predicates describing reaction. دژم گشت is a copular/resultative expression 'he became sullen/grim' (دژم = sullen, گشت = 'became'). وز پور کینه گرفت is idiomatic: وز here is an archaic connector ('from/of/and' in older usage), پور = 'son', کینه = 'hatred', گرفت = 'took/seized' (passive sense 'was seized by' or active 'took up'). Together: 'he grew grim, and the son was seized by hatred.' The verbs are simple past/resultative in narrative sequence.
segments.s378-g25: کیان شاه را گفت is a clause meaning 'he said to the Kian king' — را marks the king as the (definite) object/recipient of گفت. کای is a vocative 'O' addressing someone, and راست گوی is a nominal phrase 'truth‑speaker / one who speaks truly' that follows the vocative. In other words: 'He said to the Kian king: "O truthful one..."'.
segments.s378-g26: چنین راز گفتن کنون نیست روی is an independent clause meaning 'now it is not (appropriate/possible) to tell such a secret.' گفتن is the verbal noun 'to tell/say', کنون 'now', and نیست is the negated copula 'is not'. روی here means 'time/occasion/face' (i.e. appropriateness); thus the phrase places the denial on telling the secret at the present moment.
segments.s378-g27: سر شهریاران تهی کرد جای is a compressed, poetic word order: سر شهریاران 'the head/seat of the princes' (i.e. the position or chief place of the rulers), تهی کرد 'he made empty' and جای 'place' completes the phrase. In modern order: 'He made the place of the princes empty' (i.e. he vacated the throne/seat).
segments.s378-g28: فریبنده را گفت نزد من آی is 'he said to the deceiver: "Come to me."' فریبنده را (with را) is the direct object/recipient of گفت (i.e. 'to the deceiver he said'), نزد من 'to my presence / to me', and آی is an imperative 'come' — an archaic/impressive imperative form used poetically.
segments.s378-g29: بگوی این همه سر بسر پیش من is an imperative request: بگوی (imperative of گفتن) 'say/tell', این همه 'all these', سر بسر is an idiomatic pairing meaning 'one by one / every secret' (سر used as 'secret/inner matter' and ب‌سر here part of the idiom), and پیش من 'before me / in my presence'. The order is rhetorical/poetic but functions as 'Tell me all these things, one by one, in my presence.'
segments.s378-g30: نهان چیست زان اژدها کیش من is an interrogative: نهان چیست 'what is hidden' with چی + ست (is), where زان 'from/of that' links the following genitive phrase اژدها کیش 'the dragon's nature/character' to the question, and من is the experiencer ('from me' or 'hidden from me'). Put together: 'What of that dragon's nature is hidden from me?'
segments.s378-g31: گرزم بد آهوش گفت از خرد is compact and poetical: گرزم can be read as a name or 'my mace/club' (the lemma lists 'mace' first), بد آهوش is a descriptive phrase 'bad/ill is his gazelle' or a compound epithet, گفت is the past tense 'said', and از خرد is an adverbial phrase 'from wisdom' or 'wisely.' Grammatically, this sets up a reported speech: 'X said, wisely: ...'.
segments.s378-g32: نباید جز آن چیز کاندر خورد is a general modal statement: نباید is the impersonal modal 'one must not / it ought not', جز آن چیز 'except that thing', and کاندر خورد is a relative/adverbial clause 'which/that which happens/occurs in it' (کاندر = 'in which/therein', خورد = here 'to befall/happen'). The construction restricts permissible action/thought to only what 'occurs therein.'
segments.s378-g41: بسی لشکر آمد به نزدیک اوی = literal word order is emphatic: 'بسی لشکر' (a great/ many-host) is the subject, followed by the verb 'آمد' (came). 'به نزدیک اوی' places the direction/goal after the verb: 'to/near him.' 'اوی' is an older/pronounced form of the 3rd-person pronoun attached here as the goal of motion.
segments.s378-g42: جهانی سوی او نهادست روی = inverted, poetic order. 'جهانی' (= the world) is subject, 'سوی او' gives the direction 'toward him', and 'نهادست روی' is a compound: 'نهادست' (is set/has been placed) + 'روی' (face) = 'the face is set/turned'. The verb 'نهادست' is a stative/poetic perfective meaning 'has set/placed'.
segments.s378-g43: بر آنست اکنون که بندد ترا = 'بر آنست' is an idiom meaning 'he is bent/intent on that'; 'اکنون' simply 'now'. The following 'که' introduces a subordinate clause whose verb 'بندد' is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive form meaning 'to bind' (here 'intend to bind' or 'to put in bonds/hold'). 'ترا' is the object (2nd-person enclitic) placed after the verb in poetry.
segments.s378-g44: به شاهی همی بد پسندد ترا = 'به شاهی' = 'to/for kingship' (dative/goal). 'همی' is an archaic/emphatic particle (here reinforcing the verb). The verb complex 'بد پسندد' (poetic order) means 'finds/considers (you) fitting' — Persian often allows an adverb/particle before the verb. 'ترا' again is the 2nd-person object enclitic. Overall: 'he finds you fit for kingship.'
segments.s378-g45: تراگر به دست آورید و ببست = the clause begins with the conditional particle combined with the object: 'تراگر' = 'اگر ترا' (if [they take] you). 'به دست آوردن' is an idiom 'to seize/capture' ('دست' = hand + 'آورید' 3rd-plural present/optative 'they obtain/bring in'). The conjunction 'و' links the coordinated action 'ببست' (bound/tied). The verbs are in poetic/classical forms and the object 'ترا' precedes 'اگر' as is common in older Persian.
segments.s378-g46: کند مر جهان را همه زیردست = 'کند' is the 3rd-person singular present/future 'he makes/ will make'; 'مر' is an archaic form of the 1st-person object 'me' (to me/for me); 'جهان را همه' = 'the whole world' (object marked by 'را'); 'زیردست' is a predicative complement meaning 'under (one's) control' or 'subordinate'. Word order is verb – indirect object – direct object – complement, typical of classical Persian poetry.
segments.s378-g47: تو دانی که آنست اسفندیار = simple declarative: 'تو دانی' ('you know'), followed by a content clause introduced by 'که' ('that') with predicate 'آنست اسفندیار' = 'that is Esfandiyar.' 'آنست' is copula 'is' attached to the demonstrative 'آن' (that).
segments.s378-g48: که اورا به رزم اندرون نیست یار = a relative/explanatory clause: 'که' = 'who/for whom', 'اورا' is the object pronoun 'him', 'به رزم اندرون' = 'in close battle' or 'in inner combat' (literally 'to/for battle inside'), 'نیست' = 'there is not/does not have', and 'یار' = 'companion/ally.' Together: 'who has no companion in close combat.'
segments.s378-g17: که چون here is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'that/when' (که + چون = 'when, namely that') introducing a subordinate clause. پور is the clause's subject 'a son'; با سهم is a prepositional phrase 'with (his) share' modifying پور; و مهتر شود is the verb phrase: مهتر 'the chief/elder' is a predicative noun and شود 'becomes' is the present/future modal of شدن. Word order is subject + circumstantial phrase + predicate (typical of Persian subordinate clauses).
segments.s378-g18: ازو باب را روز بتر شود is a result/continuation clause: ازو 'from him/because of him' marks the cause; باب را is a definite object/target phrase (باب here denotes an affected party or locus — see note) and را is the object/topic marker; روز بتر شود literally 'the day becomes worse' (روز = day/fortune, بتر = worse) with شود 'becomes' as the copular/auxiliary verb. The overall sense: 'because of him the (father/household/condition) will have a worse day.'
segments.s378-g19: رهی is an indefinite/general noun 'a wayfarer' or 'one on the road' used here as 'one who...'; کز is the relative particle 'who/that' (archaic contraction of که + ز = 'that from/ who'); خداوند is the referent ('the lord/sovereign' in this context); سر برکشید is a past tense/simple perfect form 'lifted up his head' (سر = head; برکشید = lifted up). The clause is a relative clause modifying رهی.
segments.s378-g20: از اندازه‌اش 'beyond his measure' uses از to mean 'from/beyond'; اندازه‌اش is a possessive noun 'his measure/degree'; سر is the direct object 'the head'; بباید + برید expresses obligation: بباید 'must/ought' followed by برید 'cut' (here intended as 'be cut off' — note the verb form is an obligation construction). The literal order is 'from his measure head must be cut', i.e. 'his head must be cut off for going beyond his bounds.'
segments.s378-g21: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; از رازدار این شنیدم literally 'I heard this from the confidant' (از + agent, then the object این before the verb شنیدم — Persian commonly places pronouns/objects before the verb); نخست 'at first/initially' is an adverb placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s378-g22: نیامد مرا is an impersonal idiom 'did not occur to me' or 'did not come to me' (نیامد = 'did not come', مرا = dative 'to me'); این گمانی درست is the subject noun phrase 'this correct suspicion/guess' placed after the verb (common with verbs of perception/mental occurrence). The whole line means the speaker did not at first have that thought.
segments.s378-g23: جهانجوی is the speaker's epithet 'the world-seeker' (subject); گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here introducing direct speech); این سخن چیست is an embedded question 'what is this speech?' with the copula چیــست (چی + ست) after the noun; باز 'again' is an adverb placed after the clause for emphasis.
segments.s378-g24: خداوند این راز که وین چه راز reads as an address/brief interrogation: خداوند 'O lord' (or 'the lord'), این راز 'this secret', که linking/introducing the explanatory clause, وین 'this/that' (archaic demonstrative), چه راز 'what secret?'. The repetition of راز and the particles gives a rhetorical, emphatic question: 'This secret — what is this secret?'
segments.s378-g33: مرا (م + را) is the object pronoun 'me' + object marker; شاه is the (omitted) subject 'the king' and کرد is the past/light verb 'made/did' (here 'made (me) ...'). از جهان بی‌نیاز is a prepositional phrase 'from the world, without need' meaning 'free from worldly want'. The object appears before the verb (مرا ... کرد), a normal classical Persian word order.
segments.s378-g34: سزد‌گر = سزد + گر, a conditional/concessive formula 'if/should it be fitting' (classical usage). ندارم is present negative 'I do not have / I shall not receive'. بد از شاه باز here means 'badness/ill in return from the king' (باز in this context is an archaic sense 'return/recompense'); word order places the negated possession (ندارم) before the source phrase 'از شاه باز'.
segments.s378-g35: ندارم من places the negated verb before the emphatic subject 'من' ('I do not have'). از شاه خود means 'from my king' or 'from the king of mine' (خود = 'own'). باز modifies پند as 'again/further' (i.e. 'no more advice/counsel'); پند is the direct object of ندارم (object marker را is omitted).
segments.s378-g36: وگرچه is the concessive 'and/although'. The sequence مرا او را is a classical emphatic/double-object pattern (مرا with the object marker را and an additional او را to identify/emphasize the person) and functions as the object of نیاد پسند. نیاد پسند is a fixed classical collocation meaning 'is not to his liking' or 'does not please him' (نیاد here = 'disposition / come to be pleasing'); the clause means 'although he does not find me pleasing.'
segments.s378-g37: که‌گر is the conditional 'if'. راز is the object 'a secret'. گویمش is the 1st-person present/subjunctive form گویم ('I tell') plus the enclitic -ش 'to him/her' = 'I tell him'. و introduces the next clause; او نشنود is a classical negative present/subjunctive 'he does not hear' (نشنود = 'does not hear / will not hear'). Overall: 'If I tell him a secret and he does not hear (it)...'.
segments.s378-g38: به از ... is a comparative formula 'better than ...'. راز کردنش is a deverbal noun phrase: راز + کردن (to reveal the secret) with the enclitic -ش = 'its revealing' or 'revealing it'. پنهان شود is a passive/impersonal construction 'become hidden' (شود = 'become / may be'); thus the clause says it is better that the secret be kept hidden than that it be revealed.
segments.s378-g39: بدان is the imperative 'know / be aware'. ای شهنشاه is the vocative 'O king of kings' (shahanshah), addressing the sovereign; کاسفندیار (Kâspandiyâr) is the proper name in apposition — the speaker is invoking/mentioning him after addressing the king.
segments.s378-g40: بسیچد is a poetic/orthographic variant (here from چیدن 'to array/arrange' with بسی 'much/often') meaning 'he often arrays/sets in order'. همی is the habitual/aspective particle 'regularly/often'. رزم را is the object 'the battle' (را marks definiteness). روی کار is a prepositional/idiomatic phrase 'onto/into the affair' i.e. 'into action / into effect' (روی = 'on/toward', کار = 'work/affair').
segments.s378-g9: نشسته بد او پیش فرخنده شاه is a verbless/nominal clause where نشسته (a past-participial adjective ‘seated’) + بد (here an adverbial sense ‘ill/poorly’) describe the subject او. The phrase پیش فرخنده شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of / before the fortunate king’ that locates the action. Word order is typical classical Persian: predicate/adjective (نشسته بد) precedes the pronoun او and the locative phrase پیش + NP.
segments.s378-g10: رخ از درد زرد و دل از کین تباه is two coordinated nominal phrases describing condition: رخ از درد زرد (‘the face yellow from pain’) uses از + cause (درد) to mark reason and places the adjective زرد after the head رخ. The conjunction و links the second phrase دل از کین تباه (‘the heart ruined by hatred’), where تباه is a predicative adjective meaning ‘ruined’. This is standard Persian causative/ablative use of از and adjective predication.
segments.s378-g11: فراز آمد از شاهزاده سخن is an intransitive clause where آمد (past tense ‘came/rose’) takes فراز as an adverbial/verb adjunct meaning ‘up/on high’ (i.e., ‘rose’), and از شاهزاده سخن indicates the origin/agent of the utterance (‘a speech from the prince’). The word order places the circumstantial element (فراز آمد) before the source phrase از شاهزاده and the noun سخن as the theme.
segments.s378-g12: نگر تا چه بد آهو افگند بن is an imperative/request followed by a purpose/indirect interrogative clause: نگر (imperative ‘look!’) + تا introduces the indirect question ‘to see what…’ (تا چه). چه بد = ‘what bad/evil’ and افگند is the past tense ‘cast/threw/caused’. بن functions here as a postposed nominal (literally ‘base/bottom’) and is best read as the object/goal of افگند (‘has thrown down / wrought upon the base’); overall: ‘Look and see what ill the gazelle has wrought.’
segments.s378-g13: هوازی یکی دست بر دست زد: هوازی is an epithet (here ‘a free‑spirited/airy one’), یکی = ‘a certain’, and the verb phrase دست بر دست زد literally ‘struck hand upon hand’ (i.e., clapped or struck his hands). زد is simple past ‘struck’. The sequence is subject → numeral/indefiner → verbal phrase, common in narrative Persian.
segments.s378-g14: چو دشمن بود گفت فرزند بد is structured as a comparative/circumstantial clause + main clause: چو دشمن بود (‘as if he were an enemy’—چو + nominal + بود, past be) followed by گفت used here as a verb ‘he said/shouted’. The quoted or reported utterance follows (فرزند بد ‘bad/wicked child’). Note: the supplied sense list for گفت in the word data does not include the verbal sense ‘said’, so that sense is not indexed in the provided list.
segments.s378-g15: فرازش نباید کشیدن به پیش is a reported command/obligation with an impersonal modal: نباید ‘must not/should not’ + verbal noun کشیدن ‘to drag/pull’ constructs the prohibition. فرازش functions as the object/topic (‘him’ / ‘from him’ / ‘with respect to him’) and به پیش = ‘before / into the presence (of the king)’. The clause therefore means roughly ‘He must not be dragged forward / brought before [the king].’
segments.s378-g16: چنین گفت آن موبد راست کیش is a reporting clause: چنین گفت ‘thus said’ (چنین = ‘thus/so’ + گفت = ‘said’) followed by the appositive identifying the speaker آن موبد راست کیش (‘that mōbed/priest of the true/right faith’). راست kiش is an adjectival compound meaning ‘orthodox / right‑faithed’. As above, the provided sense list for گفت does not contain the verbal sense, so it is indexed as unavailable.
segments.s378-g1: یکی روز = 'one day' (indefinite temporal phrase) placed before the verb as is normal in Persian narrative. بنشست is the simple past 'sat down'. کی here functions as a temporal/relative particle 'when' linking to the following nominal 'شهریار' (the king) — overall: 'one day the king sat down.'
segments.s378-g2: به رامش بخورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'partook/ate/drank for repose' where به + noun expresses purpose ('for relaxation') and بخورد is the past (narrative) sense of خوردن 'to eat/drink/partake'. او is the pronoun 'he' (subject understood with the verb), and می‌خوش‌گوار modifies the thing taken (literally 'pleasant wine/pleasant to the taste').
segments.s378-g3: یکی سرکشی بود is an existential statement: یکی 'a certain' + سرکشی (literally 'rebelliousness' but used as a characterization, 'a rebel' or 'a headstrong fellow') + بود 'there was/was'. نامش = نام + ش (possessive clitic) 'his name', so نامش گرزم = 'his name was Garzam.'
segments.s378-g4: گوی here functions as a reportive/attributive particle 'they call / so-called' (poetic usage). نامجو can be read as an epithet 'fame-seeking' (or a byname), آزموده 'tried/experienced' modifies him, and به رزم = 'in battle/at war'. The whole phrase characterizes him as 'called (or called himself) a fame-seeker, proven in battle.'
segments.s378-g5: به دل = 'in (his) heart' (preposition + noun). کین 'vengeance/hatred' is the thing possessed; همی داشت uses همی (an older, continuous/emphatic marker) with داشت (past of داشتن) to mean 'he kept/had continually.' ز + اسم indicates the object/target of the hatred: 'toward/from Esfandiyar.'
segments.s378-g6: ندانم is the simple negative first-person 'I do not know.' چه شان بود = 'what (his) rank/standing was' where چه = 'what' and شان here means 'status/dignity' rather than the possessive clitic. از آغاز کار = 'from the beginning of the affair' (از = from; آغاز کار = the start of the matter).
segments.s378-g7: به هر جای = 'to/at every place' (preposition + هر جای). کاواز is a contracted/poetic sequence (likely کا + آواز = 'where/that the voice/fame') meaning 'where his voice/fame reached'; او 'his/it' refers back to the man, and آمدی is the past 'came/reached' (poetic word order places the verb after the referent).
segments.s378-g8: ازو = 'of him/about him' (prepositional object). زشت گفتی literally 'they said ugly things' (i.e. spoke ill), with گفتی here used as past 'said' in a narrative sense. و = 'and', طعنه زدی = 'struck taunts' i.e. 'taunted him' (زدی used idiomatically with طعنه 'to utter or inflict taunt').
segments.s378-g57: نخورد is the past‑tense negative form (ن + خورد) meaning 'did not eat' (3rd singular). ایچ is an emphatic poetical negator 'nothing/at all' placed after the verb for emphasis. می‌نیز is an archaic/intensifying particle ('too/also' or 'even') reinforcing the negation; و simply links the two clauses. رامش is an abstract noun 'tranquility/solace' serving as the object of نکرد (کرد with negative ن implicit in the preceding نکرد), so رامش نکرد = 'he found no rest/caused no peace.'
segments.s378-g58: ابی modifies بزم and is a color/quality adjective ('blue/pale') in front of the noun (classical word order). بزم بنشست is a nominal phrase plus verb: بزم (the assembly/banquet) + بنشست ('sat down' past 3rd singular); syntactically this describes the scene where the speaker sits. با introduces the accompanying instrument/companion 'with' and باد سرد is a simple noun+adjective 'cold wind.'
segments.s378-g59: از + اندیشگان is a prepositional phrase ('from/among the thoughtful' or 'to the thoughtful' in sense 'with respect to the thoughtful'); in this context it marks the person affected. نامد is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'did not come' (contracted/nearly equivalent to نیامد) and governs خواب ('sleep') as its subject/object: literally 'sleep did not come that night to the thoughtful.' آن شبش places 'that night' after the verb phrase (classical ordering).
segments.s378-g60: ز here is a poetic preposition meaning roughly 'because of / upon' introducing the experiencer. اسفندیارش is the proper name with the enclitic possessive/3rd‑person clitic (here marking 'his Esfandiar' or simply 'Esfandiar' as the person affected). گرفته is a past participle 'taken/seized' used predicatively with شتاب (i.e. شتاب گرفته = 'seized by haste'). شتاب is the noun 'haste' and together they mean 'haste had seized (him).' This is a compact classical construction where the participle follows the cause/experiencer.
segments.s378-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' از کوهساران is a prepositional phrase 'from/out of the mountains' giving the source/place of the dawn. سپیده is the noun 'dawn/first light' and دمید is the past intransitive verb 'dawned/rose' (classical poetic verb for the coming of day). Word order places the time/source phrase before the verb as is common in older Persian.
segments.s378-g62: فروغ ستاره is a genitive/attributive phrase 'the radiance/light of the star.' ببد is an archaic form of the copula/verb 'to be / to become' (classical past), here linking the subject to the adjective that follows. ناپدید is an adjective/verb meaning 'vanished/unseen.' Together the clause says literally 'the star's radiance became vanished' → 'the star's light disappeared.'
segments.s378-g63: بخواند is a past tense verb here meaning 'he called/summoned' (classical usage where خواندن can mean 'to call'). آن جهاندیده جاماسپ را is the definite object phrase: آن (that) + جهاندیده (worldly‑wise, experienced) + جاماسپ (the proper name) followed by the object marker را, so the whole is 'he summoned that experienced Jamasp.'
segments.s378-g64: کجا is the interrogative 'where.' بیش here functions as an intensifier 'anymore/else' (i.e. 'where else'). دیدست is an archaic/contracted perfect/past form meaning 'had seen' (classical usage) and takes the following object; in the printed lemma list its gloss did not include 'see,' so the exact sense is irregular/archaic. لهراسپ را is the object 'Lohrasp' marked by را. Together: 'Where else had Lohrasp seen (him)?' — an elliptical question directed to Jamasp.

sentences.s378-s41: He became wise, took the king's letter, and entrusted it to a swift rider across mountain and plain.
sentences.s378-s33: He said to him, “Go and present yourself before Esfandiyar.”
sentences.s378-s34: Tell him to rise and come to me; when you read the letter, do not set foot on the road.
sentences.s378-s35: For it is a great deed before Indra; by your foot you will traverse all these lands.
sentences.s378-s36: There is one task now that must be done: without you such a deed cannot be achieved.
sentences.s378-s37: He wrote a single firm inscription which began, "O renowned, fortunate Esfandiyar."
sentences.s378-s38: I sent that elder Jamasp so that he might carry out King Lohrasp's order.
sentences.s378-s39: When you see him, shut the middle (passage); do not hesitate — bring him on the steed Navand.
sentences.s378-s40: If you are asleep, quickly get up to the turret on foot; but if you yourself can rise, do not linger even for a moment.
sentences.s378-s25: When that lasso made a ring and writhed, it would not let the sun enter.
sentences.s378-s26: Now, from what I have heard I told you the truth; now the counsel and command are yours, know this.
sentences.s378-s27: If I, together with the Shah of Iran, strike with my mace, this will drive him away; say (it), the famed man will remain staring.
sentences.s378-s28: Thus he said: never has one seen this wonder; he became grim, and the son was seized by hatred.
sentences.s378-s13: He said to the Kian king, "O truth‑speaker, now is not the time to tell such a secret."
sentences.s378-s14: He vacated the place of the princes, and to the deceiver he said, "Come to me."
sentences.s378-s15: Say all these things one by one before me; what of that dragon's nature is hidden from me?
sentences.s378-s16: Gorzam (or his mace) — the ill gazelle — said, from wisdom: 'One ought not, except that thing which occurs therein.'
sentences.s378-s21: A vast host came up close to him; the world turned its face toward him.
sentences.s378-s22: He is intent now to bind you; he also regards you as fit for kingship.
sentences.s378-s23: If they capture you and bind you, he will make the whole world subject to me.
sentences.s378-s24: You know that that is Esfandiyar, who has no companion in close combat.
sentences.s378-s9: That when a son, with his share, becomes chief, from him his father's day (or the household's fortunes) grows worse.
sentences.s378-s10: Anyone who lifts his head against the lord (i.e. sets himself up against his sovereign) must have his head cut off beyond his due measure.
sentences.s378-s11: When I first heard this from the confidant, this correct suspicion did not come to me.
sentences.s378-s12: The world‑seeker said again, 'What is this speech?' — 'O lord, this secret — what sort of secret is this?'
sentences.s378-s17: The king has made me free from worldly need; if it be fitting, I will not receive an ill return from the king.
sentences.s378-s18: I have no further counsel from my own king, and although he does not find me pleasing, ...
sentences.s378-s19: If I tell him a secret and he does not hear it, it is better that the secret's being revealed be kept hidden.
sentences.s378-s20: Know this, O king of kings Kâspandiyâr: he oft arrays the battle and brings it into action.
sentences.s378-s5: He sat before the fortunate king in a sorry state; his face pale with pain and his heart ruined by hatred.
sentences.s378-s6: A speech rose from the prince; “Look and see what ill the gazelle has wrought.”
sentences.s378-s7: A certain free‑spirited fellow struck his hands together; like an enemy he cried out, “Wicked child!”
sentences.s378-s8: “He must not be dragged forward,” thus said that mōbed of the true faith.
sentences.s378-s1: One day the king sat down; he took a pleasant drink for relaxation.
sentences.s378-s2: There was a certain rebellious man; his name was Garzam — called a seeker of fame, proven in battle.
sentences.s378-s3: He continually harbored hatred in his heart toward Esfandiyar; I do not know what his standing was from the start of the affair.
sentences.s378-s4: Wherever his fame/voice went, people spoke ill of him and taunted him.
sentences.s378-s29: He ate nothing at all and found no repose; he sat at the pale banquet with the cold wind.
sentences.s378-s30: That night sleep did not come to the thoughtful; haste had seized Esfandiar.
sentences.s378-s31: When dawn rose over the mountains, the star's light vanished.
sentences.s378-s32: He summoned that worldly‑wise Jamasp and asked, 'Where else has Lohrasp seen him?'
